name: Juturna
description: Juturna was a Roman goddess of springs, wells, and fountains. Her name is derived from 'iuvare,' meaning 'to help' or 'aid,' reflecting her role as a beneficent deity associated with healing and purification through water. She was particularly venerated near the Lacus Juturnae in the Roman Forum, a spring believed to possess medicinal properties. Legend states that Jupiter, enamored by her beauty, granted her immortality and dominion over all the waters in Latium. Another myth tells of Juturna's tragic love affair with Janus, the two-faced god, which ended in sorrow. She is often depicted as a beautiful nymph or woman pouring water from a vase. Juturna played a crucial role in the Roman pantheon, ensuring the availability of fresh water, vital for life and health. She was also associated with childbirth and healing, making her a significant figure in Roman daily life and religious practices. Her influence extended to military affairs as well, where the waters of Juturna were believed to have aided the Roman army in battle.
subtitle: Goddess of springs, wells, and fountains

keyMyths.title: Jupiter's Love
keyMyths.description: Jupiter fell in love with Juturna and granted her immortality and dominion over the waters of Latium.

keyMyths.title: The Lacus Juturnae
keyMyths.description: The Lacus Juturnae in the Roman Forum was dedicated to her and believed to have healing properties.

keyMyths.title: Turnus's Sister
keyMyths.description: In Virgil's Aeneid, Juturna is depicted as the sister of Turnus, king of the Rutulians, and actively aids him in his war against Aeneas.

sources.author: Ovid
